• Time-restricted feeding improves cognitive function in Alzheimer’s mice through GCK/NPY/apoptosis pathway

Journal of Nutritional Biochemistry 153 (2026) 110330
RESEARCH PAPER
Time-restricte d fee ding improves cognitive function in Alzheimer’s mice through
GCK/NPY/apoptosis pathway
Zhijun Zhang
a , #
, Jingyun Yang
a , #
, Zhili Niu
b , #
, Yingna Mei
a
, Ling Gao
a , ∗
a
Department of Endocrinology & Metabolism, Renmin Hospital of Wuhan University, Wuhan, Hubei, China
b
Department of Clinical Laboratory, Renmin Hospital of Wuhan University, Wuhan, Hubei, China
Received 7 November 2025; received in revised form 9 February 2026; accepted 22 February 2026
Abstract
Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is characterized by progressive cognitive decline, neuronal loss, and circadian disruption. Time-restricted feeding (TRF), a
dietary intervention that aligns feeding with circadian rhythms, has shown potential beneﬁts in metabolic and neurodegenerative disorders. Materials and
methods: Wild-type and APP/PS1/Tau transgenic mice were divided into two groups: ad libitum and TRF, with food provided from Zeitgeber Time (ZT) 14
to ZT24 for TRF. Cognitive performance was assessed using the Morris water maze, Novel object recognition, and Rotarod tests at 3 months post-treatment.
Hippocampal tissue was analyzed by Western blot and quantitative real-time Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR). Nissl staining was used for hippocampal
morphology assessment. The glucokinase (GCK)/neuropeptide Y (NPY)/apoptosis pathway was studied by treating HT22 cells with A β and siGCK. Results:
TRF reduced food intake and alleviated weight gain, while also improving cognitive function, as evidenced by behavioral tests. Furthermore, TRF decreased
the accumulation of p-Tau protein and reduced hippocampal neuronal apoptosis, which was mediated through the restoration of the GCK/NPY pathway. In
vitro , silencing GCK in an AD cell model led to a reduction in p-Tau levels, an increase in NPY expression, and a reduction in neuronal apoptosis. Notably,
when GCK was silenced in the AD cell model, p-Tau levels and neuronal apoptosis recurred. Conclusions: Our ﬁndings suggest that TRF improves cognitive
function and reduces neuronal apoptosis in AD mice by modulating the GCK/NPY pathway. These results highlight TRF as a potential nonpharmacological
intervention for AD, offering new insights into its underlying mechanisms.
©2026 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
Keywords: Alzheimer’s disease; Time-restricted feeding; Glucokinase; Neuropeptide Y; Apoptosis.
1. Introduction
Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is a degenerative disorder of the cen-
tral nervous system characterized by the deposition of amyloid-
beta (A β) amyloid proteins and p-Tau, leading to chronic inﬂam-
mation, oxidative stress, neuronal damage, and functional im-
pairment. Circadian rhythm is a phenomenon where an organ-
ism’s physiological, biochemical, and behavioral activities exhibit
a rhythmic pattern of approximately 24 h. Study found that cir-
cadian disruption can occur in the early stages of AD, with sleep
disturbances present in 25–40% of mild-to-moderate AD patients
[ 1 ].
Lifestyle changes, such as time-restricted feeding (TRF), have
been studied for their effectiveness in restoring circadian rhythms.
TRF refers to limiting eating to a ﬁxed time window while abstain-
∗
Corresponding author at: Ling Gao, M.D., PhD, Department of En-
docrinology & Metabolism, Renmin Hospital of Wuhan University, Jiefang
RD #238, Wuhan, Hubei, 430060, China.
E-mail address: ling.gao@whu.edu.cn (L. Gao) .
#
These authors contributed equally.
ing from food during other times. This eating pattern has been
shown to reduce body weight and improve metabolism [ 2 ]. Studies
have found that TRF can reduce cell apoptosis, rescue brain pathol-
ogy in AD mice, and improve memory and other cognitive func-
tions [ 3 ]. Existing studies have found that TRF can regulate the ex-
pression of both GCK and NPY [ 4 , 5 ].
Glucokinase (GCK), also known as hexokinase 4, belongs to the
hexokinase family. In the human body, GCK is primarily concen-
trated in the pancreas, liver, brain, and gastrointestinal tract. Stud-
ies have shown that the expression of GCK is inﬂuenced by feeding
and exhibits circadian rhythmic ﬂuctuations. Typically, GCK activ-
ity increases postprandially but decreases during fasting periods,
which helps in processing glucose absorbed from food [ 4 ]. In ad-
dition, GCK is a key component of the neuronal glucose-sensing
mechanism and is expressed in brain regions that control a range
of homeostatic processes. Although there is limited research on
GCK in central nervous system degenerative diseases, studies have
found that the activation of GCK is associated with neuronal apop-
tosis [ 6 ].
Neuropeptide Y (NPY) is a neuropeptide closely associated with
the regulation of appetite, widely distributed in the central nervous
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jnutbio.2026.110330
0955-2863/© 2026 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
2 Z. Zhang, J. Yang, Z. Niu et al. / Journal of Nutritional Biochemistry 153 (2026) 110330
Fig. 1. Time-restricted feeding (TRF) and hippocampal tissue collection schedule.
(A) In the TRF group, food pellets were provided from ZT14 to ZT24.
system, where it plays a critical role in regulating various physio-
logical processes, including appetite, stress response, and neuronal
apoptosis. Research has shown that NPY can reduce neuronal dam-
age by decreasing the accumulation of A β plaques and tau tangles.
Additionally, NPY plays a crucial role in regulating synaptic plastic-
ity and neuronal survival. It helps counteract neuronal loss by in-
hibiting apoptosis and promoting neurogenesis, ultimately improv-
ing the cognitive function associated with AD [ 7 ]. Interestingly, the
expression of NPY is inﬂuenced by GCK. Inhibition of GCK can in-
crease the expression of NPY in the brain [ 8 ].
We hypothesize that TRF improves AD-related cognitive deﬁcits
by modulating the GCK/NPY pathway and ameliorating neuronal
apoptosis. Using an in vivo AD mouse model, we demonstrate that
TRF reduces p-Tau levels and neuronal damage. Furthermore, we
provide direct causal evidence by silencing GCK in HT22 cells.
2. Materials and methods
2.1. Animals and feeding regimens
APP/PS1/Tau transgenic mice (AD mice) were procured from the
Jackson Laboratory in the United States. Male C57BL/6 J wild-type
mice (WT) were obtained from the Animal Experiment Center of
Renmin Hospital of Wuhan University. During the experimental pe-
riod, the housing conditions were maintained at 23 ±2 °C, with a
12:12 light-dark cycle (lights on from 6:00 AM to 6:00 PM), and
all mice had ad libitum (AL) access to food and water until the age
of 3 months.
At the age of 3 months, male AD mice and WT mice were ran-
domly assigned to two groups: free access to food and water AL
and TRF. This resulted in four experimental groups: WTAL ( n = 18
per group), WTTRF, ADAL, and ADTRF. In the TRF group, an un-
limited quantity of food pellets was provided AL from Zeitgeber
Time 14 (ZT14, 10:0 0 PM) to ZT24 (8:0 0 AM), while water access
was unrestricted ( Fig. 1 ). This feeding regimen was maintained for
a duration of 3 months. Throughout the intervention period, the
daily food intake of the four groups of mice was measured. The ini-
tial weight of the mice from all four groups, which were 3 months
old, was recorded, and subsequent weights were taken monthly.
All animal experiments were conducted in compliance with the
guidelines set forth by the Laboratory Animal Welfare & Ethics
Committee of Renmin Hospital of Wuhan University and were ap-
proved under an animal license.
2.2. Behavioral testing
Behavioral experiments were conducted on a subset of mice
from each group ( n = 9 per group) at the age of 6 months, follow-
ing the completion of dietary interventions. All experiments were
conducted during the light phase.
2.2.1. Novel object recognition test
The novel object recognition experiment is used to assess short-
term non-spatial memory, following previously reported methods
[ 9 ]. In the training phase, two identical objects (A1 and A2) were
introduced for 10 min of free exploration. After 3 h, one object (A2)
was replaced with a new object (B) of a different shape and color,
and the mice explored for another 10 min. The exploration time
for object A2 and B was recorded, and a preference index for the
novel object (B) was calculated as the time spent with B divided
by the total exploration time (A2 + B).
2.2.2. Rotarod test
Following previous research protocols [ 1 ], mice were placed on
the rotating rod of a fatigue rotarod apparatus. The rotation speed
was gradually increased from 4 to 40 rpm within 5 min. The la-
tency to fall from the rod was recorded for each mouse. The test
consisted of three trials with a half-hour interval between each
trial. After each mouse’s completion of the test, the equipment was
cleaned with 75% alcohol.
2.2.3. Morris water maze test
The Morris water maze is a circular pool ﬁlled with a white,
milk-based liquid (22–24 °C) and divided into four quadrants. The
experiment was conducted following previous research protocols,
consisting of 5 days of place navigation trials and 1 d of spatial
probe trials [ 10 ]. The Smart 3.0 system was used to record the
mice’s movement trajectories, escape latency (time to ﬁnd the plat-
form), and the number of times they crossed the platform location.
2.3. Nissl staining
After the completion of behavioral experiments, the mouse
brain tissues were ﬁxed in 4% paraformaldehyde and embedded
in paraﬃn. Paraﬃn-embedded sections were subjected to deparaf-
ﬁnization and then stained with toluidine blue (provided by Pinoﬁ
Biotech
Company, Wuhan, China) for 1–2 min. Following staining,
the sections were differentiated in acetic acid to enhance contrast,
dehydrated, and then cleared in xylene. Finally, the sections were
mounted with neutral resin. Under a microscope (Nikon, Japan),
hippocampal neurons were observed, and cell counting was per-
formed using ImageJ software.
2.4. Cell culture and cell transfection
HT22 cells were maintained in high-glucose Dulbecco’s Modi-
ﬁed Eagle Medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine
serum and 1% antibiotics, in an incubator set at 37 °C with 5% CO
2
.
The culture medium was refreshed every 2 days. GCK siRNA, ob-
tained from RiboBio (Guangzhou, China), was used for cell trans-
fection. Before transfection, HT22 cells were seeded into six-well
plates. When the cell density reached approximately 50%, the GCK
siRNA and transfection reagent were mixed and introduced into
the wells, with the cells incubated for 48 h.
2.5. A β treated in HT22 cells
To establish an AD in vitro model, HT22 cells were treated with
A β [ 11 ]. Cells were seeded into six-well plates, achieving 80–90%
conﬂuence before the intervention. The A β
1-42
peptide (Maokang-
bio) was dissolved in dimethyl sulfoxide, diluted to a 400 µM so-
lution in Phosphate-Buffered Saline (PBS), and oligomerized at 4 °C
for 24 h. HT22 cells were exposed to 5 µM A β per well for 24 h in
serum-free medium.
2.6. Western Blotting analysis
The protein extracts were obtained by homogenization and cen-
trifugation of mouse hippocampal tissues and HT22 cells, and the
Z. Zhang, J. Yang, Z. Niu et al. / Journal of Nutritional Biochemistry 153 (2026) 110330 3
speciﬁc experimental process was conducted according to our pre-
vious work [ 12 ]. The primary antibodies contained β-actin, Tau, p-
Tau (Thr231), Cleaved Caspase-3, Bax, Bcl-2, Pro Caspase-3, GCK,
and NPY. The intensity of the bands was analyzed using ImageJ to
determine protein expression levels.
2.7. Statistical analysis
All statistical analyses were performed using GraphPad Prism.
Data are presented as the mean ±SD. For comparisons involving
more than two groups, one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was
used, followed by Tukey’s multiple comparisons post hoc test. For
data involving multiple time points, two-way repeated measures
ANOVA was applied. A P -value of less than .05 was considered to
indicate a statistically signiﬁcant difference.
3. Results
3.1. TRF reduces food intake and body weight in AD mice
By calculating the average food intake of the four groups
of mice every 2 weeks during the TRF intervention, the results
showed no signiﬁcant difference in food intake between the WTAL
and ADAL groups. However, the WTTRF group exhibited signiﬁ-
cantly lower food intake compared to the WTAL group ( Fig. 2 A,
P < .05), and the ADTRF group also showed a reduction in food in-
take compared to the ADAL group ( Fig. 2 A, P < .05). By comparing
the body weights of the four groups of mice from 3 to 6 months
of age, it was observed that the ADTRF group had a lower body
weight compared to the ADAL group ( Fig. 2 B, P < .05), while the
WTTRF group showed a trend of reduced body weight compared
to the WTAL group, but the difference was not statistically signif-
icant. These ﬁndings suggest that TRF can reduce food intake and
body weight in AD mice.
3.2. TRF improves cognitive function in AD mice without affecting
motor coordination
The four groups of mice underwent the Novel Object Recogni-
tion and Morris Water Maze tests at different time points to as-
sess cognitive function. Compared to the WTAL group, the ADAL
group exhibited a signiﬁcant increase in platform latency ( Fig. 2 D,
P < .001), a decrease in platform crossing ( Fig. 2 E, P < .05), and
a reduction in cognitive index ( Fig. 2 G, P < .05). However, after
TRF intervention, AD mice showed a decrease in platform latency
( Fig. 2 D, P < .05), an increase in platform crossing ( Fig. 2 E, P < .05),
and an improvement in cognitive index ( Fig. 2 G, P < .05). These re-
sults indicate that TRF can enhance memory and spatial explo-
ration abilities in AD mice. No signiﬁcant differences were ob-
served in the latency to fall from the rotarod between the WTAL,
ADAL, and ADTRF groups ( Fig. 2 F), indicating that TRF does not af-
fect motor coordination in AD mice.
3.3. TRF reduces phosphorylated Tau protein levels in AD mice
AD is characterized by degenerative changes in the central ner-
vous system, with its main pathological alterations being the de-
position of A β proteins and the formation of insoluble neuroﬁb-
rillary tangles composed of hyperphosphorylated Tau protein. We
conducted Western blot analyses to assess the levels of central hip-
pocampal p-Tau proteins in the four groups of mice. The results
revealed a signiﬁcant increase in hippocampal p-Tau protein levels
in the ADAL group compared to the WTAL group ( Fig. 3 B and C,
P < .01). After undergoing TRF intervention, AD mice showed a re-
duction in both hippocampal p-Tau protein levels ( Fig. 3 B and C,
P < .05).
3.4. TRF reduces hippocampal neuronal apoptosis in AD mice through
the GCK/NPY pathway
To assess the level of apoptosis in hippocampal neurons, we
performed Nissl staining of the hippocampus. The results revealed
that, compared to the WTAL group, mice in the ADAL group exhib-
ited loss of hippocampal neurons in the CA1-3 regions, a sparse ar-
rangement, and a decrease in Nissl bodies. However, following TRF
intervention, there was an increase in the number of CA1-3 hip-
pocampal neurons and Nissl bodies, indicating that TRF can reduce
the level of hippocampal neuronal apoptosis ( Fig. 3 A).
To explore the mechanisms underlying the reduction in hip-
pocampal neuronal apoptosis by TRF, we further evaluated the ex-
pression levels of GCK, NPY, and apoptosis-related signaling path-
ways through Western blot analysis. The results showed that, com-
pared to the WTAL group, mice in the ADAL group exhibited a sig-
niﬁcant increase in the expression levels of GCK ( P < .01), Cleaved
Caspase-3 ( P < .001) and Bax ( P < .01) in the hippocampus, along
with a signiﬁcant decrease in the expression level of NPY and Bcl-2
( P < .001). Subsequent to TRF intervention, hippocampal expression
of GCK ( P < .05), Cleaved Caspase-3 ( P < .001), and Bax ( P < .001) in
AD mice decreased compared to the ADAL group, while the ex-
pression of NPY ( P < .05) and Bcl-2 ( P < .05) increased. Pro Caspase-
3 levels in the hippocampus did not show signiﬁcant differences
among the four groups of mice ( Fig. 4 A–G).
3.5. GCK improves neuronal apoptosis through NPY in HT22 cells
Next, we established an in vitro AD model by treating the HT22
cells with A β. Compared to the NC group, the A β-treated group
exhibited increased expression of p-Tau ( P < .0 01), GCK ( P < .0 01),
Cleaved Caspase-3 ( P < .05), and Bax ( P < .05) proteins, while the ex-
pression of NPY ( P < .05) and Bcl-2 ( P < .05) was reduced. After si-
lencing GCK expression, the A β+ siGCK group showed decreased
levels of p-Tau ( P < .01), GCK ( P < .001), Cleaved Caspase-3 ( P < .05),
and Bax( P < .05), and increased levels of NPY ( P < .05) and Bcl-2
( P < .05) compared to the A β group ( Fig. 5 A–I). We further silenced
NPY expression and found that the levels of p-Tau, GCK, Cleaved
Caspase-3, Bax, NPY, and Bcl-2 were similar to those observed in
the A β group ( Fig. 5 A–I). This indicates that GCK acts on the apop-
tosis pathway through NPY and exerts a protective effect in the
AD.
4. Discussion
In this experiment, we have conﬁrmed the following ﬁndings:
(1) TRF can reduce food intake and alleviate weight gain in AD
mice. (2) TRF can improve cognitive function in AD mice. (3) TRF
can reduce the central deposition of p-Tau protein in AD mice and
reduce hippocampal neuronal apoptosis by the GCK/NPY pathway.
(4) GCK inhibition can protect from neuronal apoptosis through
NPY restoration in vitro AD cell model.
Intermittent fasting, including forms such as alternate-day fast-
ing, the 5:2 diet, and TRF, has been shown to reduce body weight,
extend lifespan, and delay the appearance of aging markers in
muscles and the gut [ 13 , 14 ]. Previous research has found that eat-
ing during the inactive phase (which is nighttime for humans and
daytime for rodents) can reduce lifespan and exacerbate high-fat
diet–induced obesity. Conversely, restricting eating to the active
phase can improve health [ 15 ]. There is also research suggesting
that the weight reduction associated with TRF is attributable to
calorie restriction, emphasizing the need to limit calorie intake
during TRF [ 16 ]. Through our intervention with TRF in AD mice, we
have once again conﬁrmed that TRF can reduce food intake and al-
leviate weight gain. Research has also suggested that feeding mice

(WT) and AD mice underwent the Morris water maze, Novel object recognition test, and Rotarod test. (A) The food intake curve of each group.
(B) The body weight of each

from day 1 to 5 among four groups in the navigation test. (E) The number of

every other day can improve long-term memory and promote hip-
pocampal neurogenesis [ 17 ]. These ﬁndings align with the results
of our experiment, where TRF intervention led to cognitive im-
provement, enhanced spatial exploration, and memory in AD mice.
After TRF, AD mice showed reduced levels of hippocampal p-Tau
protein, increased hippocampal neuronal and Nissl body levels.
Subsequently, to investigate the mechanisms underlying the im-
pact of TRF on hippocampal neuronal apoptosis, we further as-
sessed the levels of GCK. GCK is a member of the hexokinase
family and serves as a key glucose sensor in maintaining glucose
homeostasis, primarily expressed in the liver, pancreas, and brain.
Unlike other hexokinases (HK1–3), which have a high glucose aﬃn-
ity and become saturated at fasting glucose levels ( ∼5 mM), GCK
has a lower aﬃnity for glucose (EC50 ∼8–10 mM), is not inhibited
by glucose-6-phosphate, and phosphorylates glucose in a manner
that is directly proportional to the glucose concentration within a
physiological range (3–15 mM) [ 18 ]. Therefore, GCK is more sus-
ceptible to the effects of feeding. Studies have shown that after
TRF, the overall GCK levels in the liver decrease. The circadian
rhythm of GCK rises during the allowed feeding period, peaks at
the end of the feeding phase, and then gradually declines [ 4 ]. Our
study revealed that TRF similarly reduces the overall GCK levels in
the hippocampus of AD mice.
Notably, while GCK is recognized as a survival factor in pan-
creatic cells, it exhibits distinct, tissue-speciﬁc roles across differ-
ent organs. In the AD hippocampus, the elevated GCK observed in
the ADAL group may represent a maladaptive response to chronic
circadian disruption, metabolic stress, and impaired glucose sens-
ing. Therefore, the reduction of GCK by TRF likely reﬂects a shift
from pathological overexpression back toward a more physiologi-
cal state, thereby alleviating downstream apoptotic signaling. This
tissue-speciﬁc and state-dependent modulation allows TRF to ex-
ert neuroprotective effects that differ from GCK’s role in peripheral
glucose-sensing organs.
In the brain, glucose homeostasis is crucial, and its impairment
is a key pathogenic mechanism in AD [ 19 ]. GCK helps maintain
brain glucose balance [ 20 ]. However, studies have shown that ex-
cessive GCK activation can lead to neuronal death, which is consis-
tent with our ﬁnding that GCK expression is elevated in AD mice,
accompanied by increased neuronal apoptosis [ 6 ]. To further in-
vestigate this, we examined the Bcl-2/Bax/Cleaved caspase-3 sig-
naling pathway, which is closely associated with neuronal apop-

β group, and A β + siGCK group of HT22 cells. (B–H) Relative

tosis. Cleaved Caspase-3 represents the active form of Caspase-3.
Bax, when activated by apoptotic signals like ischemia and hypoxia,
promotes apoptosis by activating Caspase-3 through a series of re-
actions, whereas Bcl-2 opposes cell apoptosis via the mitochondrial
and endoplasmic reticulum pathways. Together, these proteins play
a role in regulating the apoptotic process [ 21 , 22 ]. Our results in-
dicate that in AD mice, there is an increase in the expression of
Cleaved Caspase-3 and Bax, while Bcl-2 is reduced, leading to an
increase in apoptosis. However, after TRF, the expression of GCK
decreased, and it reversed the expression of the Bcl-2/Bax/Cleaved
caspase-3 pathway.
The mechanism by which GCK inﬂuences apoptotic pathways
remains unclear. As mentioned earlier, GCK is associated with feed-
ing behavior and appetite regulation, and one possible mechanism
is through its effect on NPY. NPY, an appetite-related peptide, is
widely distributed in the brain. Studies have shown that NPY can
reduce neuronal damage by decreasing A β plaque accumulation
and tau tangles. Additionally, NPY can counteract neuronal loss
by inhibiting apoptosis and promoting neurogenesis [ 7 ]. On the
one hand, NPY expression is inﬂuenced by GCK. Study found that
intracerebroventricular administration of the nonspeciﬁc GCK in-
hibitor glucosamine has been shown to stimulate NPY secretion
[ 8 ]. On the other hand, NPY is also affected by TRF. Research has
found that 7–40 days of TRF in rats leads to an increase in NPY ex-
pression [ 5 ]. These ﬁndings align with our study. Additionally, we
examined the circadian rhythm of NPY mRNA expression. Our re-
sults showed that TRF increased the overall NPY levels in AD mice.
We also further validated the GCK/NPY/apoptosis pathway in vitro .
Collectively, our ﬁndings highlight the critical role of circa-
dian rhythm and glucose homeostasis in alleviating AD pathol-
ogy. In this context, genes associated with these two processes
may serve as a potential molecular link, with Sirtuin 1 (SIRT1) be-
ing a candidate [ 23 ]. As a calorie-sensitive deacetylase, SIRT1 inte-
grates metabolic status with circadian rhythm and has been shown
to mitigate Alzheimer’s pathology by protecting against neuronal
apoptosis and promoting cell proliferation [ 24 , 25 ]. While the direct
interaction between SIRT1 and the GCK/NPY axis was not exam-
ined in the present study, the activation of such a gene likely pro-
vides a complementary mechanism through which TRF mitigates
neurodegeneration. Future studies could further elucidate the po-
tential synergy between SIRT1 and GCK/NPY signaling.
Lastly, while our study demonstrates the beneﬁts of TRF, it did
not include a positive control group treated with FDA-approved
Alzheimer’s medications. Future research comparing TRF with
pharmacological interventions could provide further insights into
its potential synergistic effects.
In summary, our study involved nighttime TRF in 3xTgAD mice,
and we have conﬁrmed that TRF can reduce food intake and body
Z. Zhang, J. Yang, Z. Niu et al. / Journal of Nutritional Biochemistry 153 (2026) 110330 7
Fig. 6. Time-restricted feeding (TRF) alleviates cognitive impairment in Alzheimer’s disease (AD) by attenuating neuronal apoptosis via the glucokinase (GCK)/neuropeptide Y
(NPY) pathway.m TRF restores the disrupted circadian rhythm in AD mice. This intervention ultimately exerts positive effects on neurons within the hippocampus. TRF likely
acts by inﬂuencing the GCK and
NPY signaling pathways in the hippocampus. The downstream effect of this pathway is the regulation of neuronal apoptosis (programmed cell
death). Speciﬁcally, it promotes the expression of the anti-apoptotic protein Bcl-2 and suppresses the expression of the pro-apoptotic protein Bax. This shift in the Bcl-2/Bax
ratio subsequently inhibits the activation
of the key apoptosis executioner protein, Caspase-3 (represented in the diagram as CC3, Cleaved Caspase-3, its active form).
weight in AD mice. Additionally, it decreases hippocampal p-Tau
protein tangles, reduces neuronal apoptosis through the GCK/NPY
pathway ( Fig. 6 ). Ultimately, these interventions lead to an en-
hancement in cognitive function in AD mice. This research provides
new insights for future approaches to AD treatment.
Declaration of competing interests
The authors declare that there are no conﬂicts of interest.
CRediT authorship contribution statement
Zhijun Zhang: Writing – original draft, Methodology. Jingyun
Yang: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Zhili Niu:
Writing – original draft. Yingna Mei: Data curation. Ling Gao:
Writing –review & editing, Supervision.

mental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Project #

tion (Project # 2018HX0 0 03 ), and the Planned International Devel-
opment Project of Wuhan University.
